turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion this morning in Case 22-1165, Macquarie Infrastructure Corporation versus Moab Partners.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Securities and Exchange Commission Rule 10b-5b makes it unlawful to omit material facts in connection with buying or selling securities when that omission renders statements made misleading. Separately, Item 303 of SEC Regulation S-K requires companies to disclose certain information in filings with the SEC. The question in this case is whether the failure to disclose information required by Item 303 can support a private action under 10b-5b even if the failure does not render any statements made misleading. The Court holds that it cannot. Pure omissions are not actionable under Rule 10b-5b. Respondents alleges that petitioner had a duty to disclose a particular known threat or uncertainty under Item 303. Because petitioner allegedly violated its duty to disclose under Item 303, respondent brought an action under Section 10b and Rule 10b-5. The District Court dismissed the suit but the Second Circuit reversed. The Second Circuit concluded in part that petitioner’s alleged Item 303 violation alone could sustain respondent’s 10b and Rule 10b-5 claims. This Court granted certiorari to resolve disagreements among the circuits on whether a failure to make a disclosure required by Item 303 can standing alone support a private claim under 10b-5b even in the absence of an otherwise misleading statement. Rule 10b-5b makes it unlawful to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made in the light of the circumstances under which they were made not misleading. Logically and by its plain text, Rule 10b-5b does not prescribe pure omissions, which occur when a speaker says nothing in circumstances that do not give any special significance to that silence. The rule requires identifying affirmative assertions, that is statements made before determining if other facts are needed to make those statements not misleading. Statutory context confirms its reading of Rule 10b-5b. Congress knew how to impose liability for pure omissions as it did in Section 11(a) of the Securities Act of 1933, which prohibits registration statements that "omits to state a material fact required to be stated therein." Neither Congress in Section 10b nor the SEC in Rule 10b-5b mirrored Section 11(a) to create liability for pure omissions. Today the Court confirms that the failure to disclose information required by Item 303 can support a Rule 10b-5b claim only if the omission renders affirmative statements made misleading. For these and other reasons set forth in the opinion, the Court vacates and remands for further proceedings consistent with its opinion. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
